item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Neither of the two images used in this article were able to be sourced, and are assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can agree with the others that this is lacking some of the vital details that this SCP needs to become influential with the community. Ditto @Gaffney's comment: could use development in parts like the description and mostly in the middle-top section in the description, in my personal opinion.

Sustained feelings about this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
if there is any creepiness to this SCP text

SCP's don't need to be creepy at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a decent idea, but it doesn't feel particularly developed. Not sure where people get "creepy" from. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most likely.

Seems like we put more thought into a minor part of the SCP than the author did…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Then that attempt to transfer data to a different format was successful.

I suspect that the intent wasn't that you need to copy it to VHS before transferring it to a different format. That strikes me as Fridge Logic. I suspect that the author simply made a error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And it could've been captured from a copy of a tape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture isn't a VHS copy, it is a JPEG. Which is definitely a format change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Think I found it.

SCP-1250-1 and SCP-1250-2 can not be overwritten, and all attempts to transfer data to different formats have failed

Copying from one VHS to another VHS is transferring the data, but into the same format. If it was VHS to Digital, that fails.

Also note that it seems to imply that it will fail to transfer if you are directly copying from the tapes. If you transfer the data from a copy of the tapes, that can probably work, since you're not transfering from the actual anomaly any more, but a copy of the anomaly.

Also, note that if it cannot transfer data to a different fotmat, we can't see or hear anything on the tapes in the first place, or recall seeing them. It gets recorded first as a VHS, that's the format. To play it, it is then displayed as Video and Audio, AKA light and sound, not the magnetic strip of a VHS. That's technically a format change. Then, we see and hear the output, and code the information as electrical impulses and synaptic connections in our brains. That is definitely a format change.

Even if I'm reading too much into that phrase, nothing about that section prevents you from making VHS copies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aren't those still translating the data to another medium? These are magnetic tapes, isn't any method of copying them going to involve recording a playback in another medium? If direct digital duplication is impossible, that's not anomalous and not worth mentioning; all analog cassettes are like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something like a DVR could explain the latter, as it's copying the displayed images instead of transferring the data.

Or, you know, just film the output of the recording with another camera.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the original author is still around:

  • You still have an ampersand that probably ought to be an "and".
  • As pointed out upthread "all attempts to transfer data to different formats have failed" is directly contradicted by the second picture.

Staff, if Uncle Joe isn't around (and it looks like he hasn't posted any comments since 2012) I am assuming that the first one counts as a grammar fix and is a permissible edit? The second is obviously content, and off limits, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely loved the log! In particular, I found the redacted dice roll result to be simultaneously hilarious and terrifying. Something very sinister about a simple action. It was very jarring and unexpected. Brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
all attempts to transfer data to different formats have failed

Hm… but what about the 2nd picture in the article (apples and bananas)? Isn't it a successful data transfer to different format?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That second pic is utterly horrifying and the fact that the recorded permutations are not massively bat-shit-insane deviations makes this one even more disturbingly creepy and holy fuck how is this not more highly rated

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. No clue what most of the past comments are about. But I like what's here. It's neat and weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy a good bit of the article, but SCPs always leave a bad taste in my mouth when they redact utterly incongruous things just for the sake of redacting things.

Redacting the name of a children's show? Really? :

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There was originally a line in the article describing that the tapes can only be used to record a single time before they're 'spent', to prevent it from seeming like there was an unlimited potential for use. Not sure why I ended up removing that line in the end, but i'll go ahead and re-add it along with some additional clarifications to better explain things. Oh, and there's no longer a box randomly generating these samples. You probably caught an earlier revision.

Also taking your comments about the lack of hook to heart. I do like your suggestions regarding the amateur videographer, though i'm worried about making the article far to large/unfocused if I go off on a tangent about the origins of the tapes. I'll see what I can do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, thanks. I'll keep that in mind with any future submissions I make.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And now it's just the tapes, and now it gets an upvote from me! I'm taking the lesson from this SCP to heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with FuzzyBoots. It's an OK idea, however after reading the main section of the SCP report I fail to see any hook.

Upvote for the significant improvement (comparing to the original version), but it will be a lot better if you make some more adjustments.

By the way, this SCP reminds me of SCP-970

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm rather undecided on this one. It's an alright idea, but it's not gripping me, and without the experimental logs, it comes off as very bland and uninteresting. I would recommend integrating a few representative logs and then linking to the rest.

Also, the use of black boxes seems to imply over 10,000 of each of the tapes… that's a bit high, effectively unlimited, especially with re-recording and the seeming infinite length. I'm also a bit iffy on the box generating more. Easier would be to identify them as Brand X Model Y tapes, resulting in Foundation personnel using various discreet methods to locate and acquire them, along with the occasional very strange home movie (which actually would provide a nice hook for these being discovered, some amateur videographer gets in the news for reporting that he's filmed a murder, or a xenocritter, but the evidence is against him, leading to allegations of fakes and then the Foundation stepping in to relieve him of the tapes).

Incidentally, regarding re-recording, it would be interesting to note whether re-recording over the same tape results in the same reality being filmed.

No vote for now. Like I said, good idea, but the execution does not grip me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This new version is significantly better than the weird hairs thing. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And that's yet another reason why it's locked up in Foundation custody.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One more consistency concern…

"Testing has also shown that SCP-1250-1 and SCP-1250-2 samples do not appear to have any storage limit."

This seems mechanically impossible, because in order to play a video cassette, the tape itself must be drawn along the VCR's reading head. If the tapes do not have a storage limit, that means that SCP-1250-1 and -2 contain an infinite amount of tape. Which is a lot of tape to be found inside of a standard-sized cassette.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It used to be a jar that extruded hairs, which could be wound around spools and used in standard cassette and VCR devices. The test logs are exactly as they were, except I think Dr. Carter's note on the last video recording is longer than it was.

Dear author: Ampersands are extremely informal, and have no place in official Foundation documents. I've corrected that for you. Sincerely, the crazy guy with the grammar fixation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like Sorts, I seem to have only see this after the hair part has been clipped. (doooh ho ho ho)
That said, I think it's now good enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go thou not into the hairy past.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just read this for the first time and have promptly upvoted for the weirdness of it all… what was the hair stuff about in above comments? Guess I will have to check previous revisions. As it is I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback, and thanks for the catch about that formatting issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better. I'd remove the box picture just so that the formatting isn't messed up, but otherwise, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Magnetic tapes are re-usable and can be overwritten.

EDIT: Any magnetic medium is re-writable by design (as the physical structure of the medium does not change, unlike in CDs and DVDs — your hard drive operates on the same principle, and it gets re-written and over-written thousands of times a day). The only way you could make a tape copy-protected is by introducing mechanical fail-safes into both the medium and the reproduction device — that is to say, while you have to bend over assward to make CDs re-writable, you have to bend over assward to make magnetic tapes NON-rewritable.

Good luck!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In response to your comment about the box; I agree entirely that there are an abhorrent number of SCPs in the wiki that 'do a thing while no one is looking'. My main issue is that i'd like there to be/think there needs to be some means of frequently restoring the Foundation's supply of tapes in order for them to be able to carry out experiments with the objects for 15+ years.

A few other options I can think of would be for the foundation to find multiple boxes full of the tapes over the years, or for the initial discovery having been for a collection of 1,000's of tapes of each type. I feel i'd probably need to dedicate just as much, if not more, space to properly explain those situations. I'd happily take a crack at doing that, though, if you feel the article would benefit from the tapes originating by one of those means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this SCP is trying to do too little with too many words. I read the testing logs, and I absolutely loved them. I think all you need for this particular SCP is a short few paragraphs describing the tapes and nothing else — that alone will definitely earn an upvote from me. The box makes me frustrated, mainly because of the "it does things when no one is looking" bit of it. There's a metric fuckton of SCPs that already do that. Furthermore, I don't think the box adds anything to the article.

So, my suggestion: cut the article so that it's 1/3rd of its current length, have it be about the tapes and nothing else, and leave the testing logs as they are now (because right now, the logs are absolutely the best part of this SCP). As it is, the article itself is not enough — or, rather, too much — for me to upvote, but the concept is interesting enough that a downvote feels dickish. So, no vote.

EDIT: In cutting, I'd also include the addendums and the good doctor's note in italics. The note can be summarized with a few words inside the body of the description — as a note, the tone feels very off, with a doctor losing his shit like that. The addendums effectively add nothing to the article, and should instead be carried over into the testing logs, if you feel them necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback everyone. I was a bit worried about the article having too much to talk about myself, and It seems I was right. I'm starting on a revision, and I'm fairly confident that the article will do a much better job of explaining what the SCP is & how it works when I am done.

EDIT: Okay, just published those aforementioned changes. New name, new image, and new focus. Perhaps I'll do something with the jar concept at a later date, but this article is definitely better without it. Let me know what you think!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They seem to serve as a recording medium that can only record data from neighboring realities. The playback never matches what happens in the Foundation's reality, only in alternate worlds.

Intriguing, but in my opinion, not really carried far enough, nor made clear enough. (Although it would be interesting if one of the recordings included complaints from SCP-507 - who was at the time away on one of his "jaunts"…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The test logs are great. I think the main article may have an issue with too much going on. You've got a mystic jar, weird hairs, and something messing with recording devices, which is the real thing going on here. You may want to try consolidating the concept to include less so you get to the meat of the SCP with more clarity. Also, it doesn't help that I saw the picture, started reading, looked back at the picture and said, "That's not a jar!" Confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I skimmed the main article, thought I got it, then moved on to the test logs. Liked the test logs but realized I'd missed something in the main article. Had to read it like five times to get it. I get that the bowl makes the hair and the hairs make the cassettes but it is way too complicated. Simplify the whole process and maybe I'll upvote. The logs are pretty good. I'd like there to be some sort of indication if Dr. Carter remembers/dreamed about that last bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description is confusing and doesn`t really describe the actual item. Also, I don`t see how this is an SCP and why the foundation would want to keep it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the test log, but the main object is rather too waffly and yet unclearly described.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is not very good, but it may have potential. I like the central image, but it's unclear what exactly the hairs do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm, I like the basic concept, but it needs major edits for tone and a few for grammar. That said, if there is any creepiness to this SCP, I have yet to see it, and the description is a bit confusing regarding what exactly this SCP does. I'm putting a downvote on now, but if there's any major improvement I will reconsider.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering this is my first submission; I wanted to try and make something that was more interesting than it was outright horrifying, but still had the potential for sinister imagery. Any and all criticism is greatly appreciated, and I'll do what I can to improve the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hair recordings…
What!?
